name: Mozarabic Way: Castuera to Benquerencia Castle Loop
name_original: Castuera y Castillo de Benquerencia de la Serena — circular por la tierra de La Serena
description: Cycle 12.9 miles (20.7 km) on this moderate touring route from Castuera, featuring the historic Castillo de Benquerencia de la Serena.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Castuera traverse a landscape characterized by rolling plains, agricultural areas, and scattered hills. The region features large reservoirs, such as the Embalse de Orellana, which offer routes with water views. Historical sites, including castles and ancient pathways, are integrated into the cycling network, providing cultural points of interest. The terrain generally presents a mix of flat sections and moderate ascents, suitable for various touring cycling experiences. Best touring cycling routes around Castuera The most popular touring cycling route is Zona de Interés Regional Embalse de Orellana y Sierra de Pela — circular desde Orellana la Vieja, a 34.5 miles (55.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 36 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the Orellana reservoir and passes through the Sierra de Pela. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Mozarabic Way: Castuera to Benquerencia Castle Loop, a moderate 12.9 miles (20.7 km) path. This route follows a historical path, leading to Benquerencia Castle with views of the surrounding countryside. Local touring cyclists also love the Castuera Concentration Camp – Puerto Mejará loop from Castuera, a 40.0 miles (64.4 km) trail leading through varied terrain and historical points, often completed in about 6 hours 2 minutes. Touring cycling around Castuera is defined by rolling agricultural landscapes, reservoir vistas, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and challenging routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Castuera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 30 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castuera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Castuera?
A: There are nearly 10 touring cycling routes around Castuera, offering a variety of experiences from easy rides to more challenging expeditions. These routes traverse a landscape characterized by rolling plains, agricultural areas, and scattered hills.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Castuera?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Castuera is typically during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, sometimes wetter, winter months.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Castuera?
A: Yes, Castuera offers at least one easy touring cycling route. While specific easy routes are not detailed in the provided data, the region's terrain includes flat sections suitable for less experienced cyclists or families looking for a relaxed ride.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Castuera?
A: The terrain around Castuera generally presents a mix of flat sections and moderate ascents, with some routes featuring more challenging climbs. You'll encounter rolling plains, agricultural areas, and scattered hills, often with views of large reservoirs like the Embalse de Orellana.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, Castuera offers several difficult routes. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327246907/zona-de-interes-regional-embalse-de-orellana-y-sierra-de-pela-circular-desde-orellana-la-vieja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zona de Interés Regional Embalse de Orellana y Sierra de Pela — circular desde Orellana la Vieja&lt;/a&gt;, a 34.5-mile (55.5 km) trail with significant elevation changes. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20128604" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castuera Concentration Camp – Puerto Mejará loop from Castuera&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 40.0 miles (64.4 km) through varied terrain.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Castuera area?
A: Yes, there are circular touring cycling routes available. A notable example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327246907/zona-de-interes-regional-embalse-de-orellana-y-sierra-de-pela-circular-desde-orellana-la-vieja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zona de Interés Regional Embalse de Orellana y Sierra de Pela — circular desde Orellana la Vieja&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a scenic loop around the Orellana reservoir and through the Sierra de Pela.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many touring cycling routes around Castuera integrate historical sites. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022448771/mozarabic-way-castuera-to-benquerencia-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mozarabic Way: Castuera to Benquerencia Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; follows a historical path leading to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6085543" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Benquerencia de la Serena&lt;/a&gt;. Other nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena Dam&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6088024" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Columns of Zalamea de la Serena&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any routes that offer scenic views of water bodies?
A: Absolutely. The region features large reservoirs, and routes often provide stunning water views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327246907/zona-de-interes-regional-embalse-de-orellana-y-sierra-de-pela-circular-desde-orellana-la-vieja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zona de Interés Regional Embalse de Orellana y Sierra de Pela — circular desde Orellana la Vieja&lt;/a&gt;, for example, offers direct views of the Orellana reservoir. You can also visit &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5667110" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de Orellana&lt;/a&gt;, a beach area on the reservoir.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Castuera?
A: The touring cycling routes in Castuera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the integration of historical points, and the scenic reservoir vistas.

Q: How long do touring cycling routes around Castuera typically take to complete?
A: The duration of touring cycling routes in Castuera varies significantly depending on the distance and difficulty. For example, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022448771/mozarabic-way-castuera-to-benquerencia-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mozarabic Way: Castuera to Benquerencia Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes approximately 1 hour 27 minutes, while the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20128604" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castuera Concentration Camp – Puerto Mejará loop from Castuera&lt;/a&gt; can take around 6 hours 2 minutes.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through or near other towns or villages?
A: Yes, some routes connect Castuera with nearby towns and offer glimpses into local life. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38170886" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campanario (Badajoz) – Villanueva de la Serena loop from Campanario&lt;/a&gt; connects these two locations, allowing you to explore beyond Castuera itself.

Q: What should I bring for a touring cycling trip in Castuera?
A: Given the varied terrain and potential for longer rides, it's advisable to bring sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (especially during warmer months), and basic bike repair tools. A helmet is essential, and comfortable cycling attire is recommended. Always check the weather forecast before heading out.
tips: The Castuera hill is a magical and beautiful place, you can't miss it.
It is a rural road that goes from the town of Castuera to Monterrubio de La Serena, planning across a large plain in the middle of two mountain ranges.

Dividing La Serena into two areas in a north-south direction, rise the mountains of Benquerencia, Los Tiros and Almorchón. Its passage is only practicable through the Mejoral and de la Nava ports, which since time immemorial have channeled communications between the Meseta and the pastures of La Serena as an obligatory route for the cattle of the Mesta.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Castuera and Castillo de Benquerencia de la Serena cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate for touring cycling. It covers approximately 20.7 kilometers (12.9 miles) with an elevation gain of about 250 meters (820 feet), requiring good fitness. The estimated completion time is around 1 hour and 27 minutes.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily consists of mostly paved surfaces, making it accessible for various cycling preferences. However, some segments may be unpaved and potentially difficult to ride. You'll traverse a large plain situated between two mountain ranges, offering diverse landscapes.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route combines natural beauty with historical sites. You will pass by the former Castuera Concentration Camp, a site of historical significance. A major highlight is the opportunity to visit the ruins of the Benquerencia de la Serena Castle, which offers extensive views of the surrounding countryside and La Serena.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Castuera, which is a good place to find parking. As it's a circular route, you can typically find parking within the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is outdoors, it's primarily a cycling trail that includes both paved and unpaved sections. If your dog is accustomed to running alongside a bicycle and you adhere to local leash laws, it might be possible. However, consider the distance and terrain for your dog's comfort and safety.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region of Extremadura can experience very hot summers. Therefore, spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) are generally the best times to cycle this route, offering more pleasant temperatures for outdoor activities.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and trails, making it freely accessible.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino Mozárabe, Camino de Andaque, and Camino de Belalcázar a Castuera, among others.

faqs.question: Are there any places to eat or drink along the way?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Castuera, where you will find various cafes and restaurants. The route also passes near Monterrubio de la Serena, which may offer additional options.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: You can expect expansive views of the La Serena region, with dramatic contrasts between the large plain and the surrounding Sierra de Castuera and Sierra de Benquerencia mountain ranges. The Benquerencia de la Serena Castle ruins offer particularly extensive panoramic views.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the starting point in Castuera?
faqs.answer: Yes, Castuera is a town in the province of Badajoz, Spain, and can be accessed by public transport, though options might be limited depending on your starting location. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for connections to Castuera, especially if you plan to bring your bicycle.
